Several large-scale LiDAR datasets have been released for object detection.
Several large-scale LiDAR datasets have led to the proposal of many object detection networks.
VoxelNet leverages PointNet to generate a voxel-wise representation for object detection.
SECOND exploits sparse 3D convolutions to accelerate VoxelNet.
Point2Seq reformulates the 3D object detection task as decoding words from 3D scenes in an auto-regressive manner.
Due to the quantization errors of voxelization, some methods directly operate on raw point clouds for detection.
methods directly operate on raw point clouds for detection
3DSSD extends VoteNet and proposes a hybrid sampling strategy by utilizing both feature and geometry distance for better classification performance
a number of approaches take advantage of the efficiency of 3D sparse convolutions while preserving accurate point positions
FSD builds a fully sparse 3D object detector to enable efficient long-range detection
Graph R-CNN speeds up the RoI pooling in PointRCNN and introduces a graph-based refinement to achieve better performance
Transformer has become a competitive feature learning module in many computer vision tasks
Point Transformer employs vector attention to better extract local features
Point Transformer V2 enhances Point Transformer and presents a more powerful and efficient model
Fast Point Transformer proposes a lightweight self-attention layer and a voxel hashing-based architecture to boost computational efficiency
Stratified Transformer enlarges the effective receptive field at a low computational cost by sampling nearby points densely and distant points sparsely in a stratified way
Object DGCNN models 3D object detection
DGCNN models 3D object detection as message passing on a dynamic graph
OcCo occludes point clouds based on different viewpoints and learns to complete them
PointContrast contrasts point-level features from two transformed views to learn discriminative 3D representations
DepthContrast learns features by considering voxels and point clouds of the same 3D scene as data augmentations
4DContrast leverages 4D signals in unsupervised pre-training to imbue 4D object priors into learned 3D representations

In contrast to conventional voxel-based detectors, pillar-based methods discretize the input point cloud with a grid

The points are assigned to pillars to generate a feature vector. Subsequently, the obtained pillar features are scattered back to their corresponding horizontal locations in the scene to form a dense 2D pseudo-image.
The pseudo-image is then processed by a feature pyramid network, which extracts multi-scale features using convolution layers with strides of 1×, 2×, and 4×.
Similar to SwinTransformer, SST divides the space into a list of non-overlapping windows with a fixed size. The self-attention is adopted among pillars within the same window.
SST achieves impressive results for small object detection due to its single-stride property.
Due to the self-occlusion of 3D objects, most of the points are sparsely distributed over the surface of the objects.
Spatial disconnection of sparse points can be exacerbated when extensive masking is applied.
It will be challenging to use a traditional convolution backbone like PointPillars for visible points due to limited receptive fields.
Inspired by SST, a transformer-based pyramid structure is introduced to achieve a large spatial scope.
Inspired by the success of MAE in 2D images, a masked autoencoder is developed for self-supervised learning on LiDAR point clouds.
The core idea is to use the encoder to create multi-scale representations from partial observations of the LiDAR point clouds.
After pre-training, the parameters of the encoder are used to warm up the backbone of the detection task.

Directly applying the original masking strategy to the last stage of the multi-scale encoder makes the pretext task too difficult for small objects.
Different masking strategies with varied granularities are designed to make the training task from difficult to easy.
Block-wise Masking masks a portion of non-empty tokens from downsampled feature maps and tries to recover them.
The multi-scale structure requires backtracing to make the masked regions consistent across scales and avoid information leakage from previous stages.
The masking map is upsampled to its original scale to determine if pixels are masked according to the input tokens' coordinates.
Tokens are used to determine whether they are masked or not, and only the unmasked tokens are fed into the encoder.
In the case of a pedestrian, the whole body is masked out and needs to be recovered from the arms or elsewhere, making the task hard.
Patch-wise Masking adopts a smaller masking granularity than block-wise masking.
Patch-wise Masking masks some of the upsampled tokens, maintaining consistency in masking granularity.
Point-wise Masking directly masks out a number of the input point clouds and reconstructs the masked points inside tokens.
It is trivial to train a point-wise decoder to predict the coordinates of the masked points, as the positional encoding would leak the information.

GD-MAE consistently improves the accuracy of different multi-scale encoders, with the highest gain observed for the proposed transformer encoder, suggesting the suitability of the simple decoder for the transformer-based encoder.

Effect of different masking granularities on LiDAR point clouds is studied, with different masking granularity levels leading to varying levels of difficulty in the pretext task of fusing hierarchical features.
Using finer masking granularity can ease the task and consistently improve performance, especially for pedestrians.
Pre-training with block-wise masking brings gains in APH/L2 for both vehicle and pedestrian, with performance consistently improved, particularly for the pedestrian category.
